title: Why Do Crows Remember One Human Face for Years?
published_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime, set on actual publish
updated_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime
summary: Wildlife biologist John Marzluff's mask experiments at the University of Washington reveal how crows recognize individual human faces, remember threats for years, and teach that danger socially across generations — a landmark case in animal cognition and corvid behavior.
answer: TODO -- fill in before publish -- one paragraph direct answer; can adapt from script.md's cold open / closing reframe
duration_iso: TODO -- fill in before publish -- e.g. PT8M30S, fill in once the final render duration is known
key_findings: Seventeen years of memory in an animal that doesn't live much longer than that is remarkable on its own.
And there's a neurological answer for how a brain this different from ours can pull off something this similar to what we do.
Crows are not the only corvid that does this.
But the ravens added a twist Marzluff's crows had not shown.
That detail changes how we should read the crows too.
article_markdown: TODO -- fill in before publish -- fresh long-form written article for reading (NOT the spoken script verbatim). Use script.md and sources.json as source material.

sources.title: Marzluff, J.M., Walls, J., Cornell, H.N., Withey, J.C. & Craig, D.P. (2010). Lasting recognition of threatening people by wild American crows. Animal Behaviour, 79(3), 699-707.
sources.publisher: 2006-launched mask experiment; dangerous vs. neutral mask; crows scolded the dangerous mask regardless of wearer; recognition persisted at least 2.7 years

sources.title: University of Washington, Urban@UW. 'Crows hold grudges against individual humans for up to 17 years.' Institutional news coverage of the Marzluff mask-recognition research program.
sources.publisher: Neutral mask resembled Dick Cheney (then-sitting VP); scolding behavior finally stopped September 2023, seventeen years after the study started

sources.title: Cornell, H.N., Marzluff, J.M. & Pecoraro, S. (2012). Social learning spreads knowledge about dangerous humans among American crows. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 279(1728), 499-508.
sources.publisher: Social transmission -- crows never personally trapped, including those born after the original event, still scolded the mask

sources.title: Marzluff, J.M., Miyaoka, R., Minoshima, S. & Cross, D.J. (2012). Brain imaging reveals neuronal circuitry underlying the crow's perception of human faces. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 109(39), 15912-15917.
sources.publisher: PET imaging showing amygdala-analogous crow brain region activation in response to the dangerous face

sources.title: Blum, C.R., Fitch, W.T. & Bugnyar, T. (2020). Rapid Learning and Long-Term Memory for Dangerous Humans in Ravens (Corvus corax). Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 581794.
sources.publisher: Cross-species replication in ravens: captive ravens learned to distinguish a dangerous mask (paired with a dead raven) from a neutral mask, some after a single exposure, with discrimination persisting up to 4 years; alarm-calling intensity correlated with social dominance rather than memory strength, suggesting a status-signaling function alongside the threat-memory function
